beats.text: Boosting builds weak decision trees in sequence, each one trained to fix the mistakes the running total still makes.

visual.sub: Sequential trees that reduce remaining bias

beats.text: Friedman's gradient boosting machine treats the model as a point in function space and runs gradient descent on the loss.

visual.note: Stagewise additive update; earlier trees are never re-touched

beats.text: Each new tree is fit not to the labels but to the negative gradient of the loss, the steepest-descent direction.

visual.note: The pseudo-residual every tree regresses on

beats.text: For squared-error loss the negative gradient is exactly the ordinary residual, so the tree simply fits the leftover error.
Starting from the mean, a sequence of stumps drives the training error down, falling monotonically with each added tree.
The learning rate shrinks every correction, so a smaller rate means gentler steps that need more trees but generalize better.

visual.label: shrinkage applied per tree at ν = 0.1

concepts.title: AdaBoost & exponential loss — where it started

beats.text: AdaBoost predates the gradient view and works by keeping a weight on every training example, up-weighting the ones it gets wrong.
Each round fits a classifier, measures its weighted error, and assigns it a vote that grows as the error falls.

visual.note: A learner at chance gets vote zero; a near-perfect one gets a large vote

beats.text: A learner with thirty percent weighted error earns a vote of about 0.42, then errors are reweighted heavier than successes.
Misclassified points grow by about half while correct ones shrink by a third, forcing the next learner to attend to the hard cases.
Friedman, Hastie and Tibshirani showed AdaBoost is exactly forward stagewise modeling under the exponential loss.

visual.note: AdaBoost is gradient boosting with a specific loss

beats.text: Exponential loss punishes confident mistakes without bound, which makes vanilla AdaBoost sensitive to mislabeled data and outliers.
XGBoost took Friedman's algorithm and made it production-grade with two ideas: explicit regularization and second-order gradients.
Its objective adds a per-leaf cost and an L2 penalty, and uses both the gradient and the Hessian of the loss.

visual.note: g and h are the first and second derivatives; γ and λ are regularizers

beats.text: Because each leaf is a quadratic, the optimal leaf value has a closed form: the negative gradient sum damped by the Hessian.
With gradient sum minus ten, Hessian sum five, and penalty one, the optimal leaf value works out to about 1.67.

visual.label: optimal leaf value w★ for G=−10, H=5, λ=1

beats.text: A split's gain compares the children to the parent and subtracts the leaf cost, so XGBoost prunes any split whose gain is negative.

visual.note: γ is a built-in pre-pruning knob

beats.text: A small learning rate with many trees rides the held-out minimum down, while a large rate overfits as the held-out curve turns up.
LightGBM keeps XGBoost's objective but rebuilds the machinery for speed and memory at scale through three engineering bets.
Rather than scanning every distinct feature value, it buckets each feature into fixed bins and builds gradient histograms once.
With 256 bins a feature offers 255 candidate cut points, instead of one per distinct value, which is what makes the scan cheap.
Histogram binning cuts split-finding cost from order n times d down to order bins times d, dramatically faster on huge data.
LightGBM grows trees leaf-wise, always splitting the single leaf with the largest gain rather than splitting level by level.

visual.note: Lower loss per leaf budget, but more prone to overfit

beats.text: Setting num_leaves of eight against a depth-ten capacity uses under one percent of the tree, the standard guard against overfitting.

visual.label: fraction of 2^10 capacity used by num_leaves=8

beats.text: CatBoost targets a bug the others share: target leakage, which appears both in categorical encoding and in residual computation.
Mean-encoding a category leaks because the row's own label sits in the average used to encode it, letting the model peek.
Ordered target statistics encode each row using only earlier rows in a random permutation, plus a smoothing prior for rare categories.

visual.note: Row i's own label never enters its own encoding

beats.text: Earlier labels summing to two over a count of three, with prior one-half and smoothing one, give an encoding of 0.625.

visual.label: ordered target statistic for Σy=2, count=3, a=1, p=0.5

beats.text: Ordered boosting computes each row's gradient from a model that never saw that row, cutting the prediction-shift bias.
No library dominates; tuned, all three sit within noise, so you pick by data shape and constraints rather than a mythical accuracy gap.
